title: Happiness pill
population: young adults in the united states
limitations: not enough people enrolled into study, loss to followup

summary.background: A cross-sectional study was conducted to evaluate whether a 'happiness pill' intervention was associated with improved happiness scores in young adults in the United States.
summary.findings: The study found an opposite-direction result. The reported relative risk was 1.3, with a p-value of 0.5, indicating no statistically significant association between the happiness pill and happiness scores. The effect estimate does not support a beneficial effect of the intervention.
summary.main_limitation: The authors identified two limitations: insufficient enrollment of participants and loss to follow-up.

summary.background: A randomized clinical trial was conducted to evaluate a happiness score intervention in women, with the primary outcome being the happiness score itself. No additional rationale was provided.
summary.findings: The trial yielded inconclusive results. The primary outcome of happiness score showed no statistically significant effect, with a p-value of 0.40 and a hazard ratio of 1.2. These results do not support a meaningful intervention effect based on the provided statistical information.

summary.background: An observational cohort study was conducted to assess the effect of a happiness pill on happiness scores in women aged 28 and older. No further rationale was provided.
summary.findings: The study reported a null result. The primary outcome of happiness score did not reach statistical significance (p = 0.45). The effect estimate was reported as SMD: 20, though the clinical interpretation of this value is unclear given the non-significant p-value.
